coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composites at room temperature with improved visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jingjie
Liu, Quanrun
Wang, Junhao
He, He
Shi, Feng
Xing, Baolin
Jia, Jianbo
Huang, Guangxu
Zhang, Chuanxiang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have demonstrated tremendous potential in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of TiO2; however, the fabrication of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts typically involves harsh reagents/conditions, cumbersome purification procedures and external energy supplies. In this work, we present a facile and energy-efficient impregnation method for the preparation of effective CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts at room temperature employing coal tar pitch (CTP) as “natural CQDs”. The characterization results indicate that TiO2 and CQDs can be successfully coupled together by forming Ti‒O‒C bonds. The fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composites show remarkably improved photodegradation performances of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible-light irradiation, which are far higher than pure TiO2. At the optimum CQDs content (6 wt%), the degradation rate constant is 43.7-fold higher than that of pure TiO2. The marked ameliorated photocatalytic performance is attributed to the fact that the successful coupling between TiO2 and CQDs can ameliorate the absorption ability of visible light, accelerate the separation/transfer of charge carriers and restrain the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole (e−–h+) pairs. This research provides a facile method for large-scale preparation of CQDs modified semiconductor photocatalysts that can potentially be used in environmental and energy applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
TiO2
Visible-light photocatalysis
RhB degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites
Photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts
Mechanism with enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful for the financial support given by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant numbers U1361119 , and 51404098 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (the insert is the size distribution) and (b) HRTEM image of CQDs. (c) TEM image and (d) HRTEM image of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite.
Elemental mapping patterns of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite.

(a) XRD patterns of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites. (b) Raman spectra and (c) FT-IR spectra of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. (d) The full survey XPS spectrum and (e) C 1s XPS spectrum and (f) O 1s XPS spectrum of the fabricated CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. (g) Ti 2p XPS spectrum comparison of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. The insert is O 1s XPS spectrum comparison of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite.
Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distributions (inset) of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites.
(a) UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra and (b) Tauc's plots for the band gap values of different samples.

(a) Photocatalytic activity and (b) degradation efficiency (%) and (c) reaction kinetic study and (d) degradation rate constants of RhB in the existence of different photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation.

(a) Study on stability of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite photocatalyst in four continuous cycle of photodegradation experiments and (b) XRD patterns of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite photocatalyst before and after four repeated RhB degradation tests.
(a) PL spectra and (b) EIS spectra of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites.

(a) Relative RhB concentration versus degradation time without addition and addition of various radical scavengers. (b) Histogram displaying the influence of different radical scavengers on the photodegradation efficiency (%) of RhB.
The possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of RhB on CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts.
Comparisons of the results of this work with those of other recent CQDs/TiO2 photocatalysts.

row.entry: GQDs: in hot HNO3 at 80 °C for 8 h GQD/TiO2: Heated at 150 °C for 4 h
2 h, 350 W Xe lamp with a cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm)
GOQDs: chemical oxidation GOQDs-sensitized TiO2: impregnation
GOQDs: refluxing in HNO3 for 24 h GOQDs-sensitized TiO2: stirring for 12 h
4 h, 500 W Xe lamp with an UV filter (λ < 420 nm)
2.5 h, 500 W Xe lamp with an UV filter (λ > 420 nm).
1.5 h, 500 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter.
Ultrasonic for 1 h/heated at 180 °C for 12 h
6 h, 300 W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm)
Ultrasonic for 1 h/stirring for 12 h at room temperature
4 h, 300 W Xe lamp with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm)

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 composites at room temperature with improved visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454003, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Polytechnic University, Jiaozuo 454003, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have demonstrated tremendous potential in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of TiO2; however, the fabrication of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts typically involves harsh reagents/conditions, cumbersome purification procedures and external energy supplies. In this work, we present a facile and energy-efficient impregnation method for the preparation of effective CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts at room temperature employing coal tar pitch (CTP) as “natural CQDs”. The characterization results indicate that TiO2 and CQDs can be successfully coupled together by forming Ti‒O‒C bonds. The fabricated CQDs/TiO2 composites show remarkably improved photodegradation performances of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible-light irradiation, which are far higher than pure TiO2. At the optimum CQDs content (6 wt%), the degradation rate constant is 43.7-fold higher than that of pure TiO2. The marked ameliorated photocatalytic performance is attributed to the fact that the successful coupling between TiO2 and CQDs can ameliorate the absorption ability of visible light, accelerate the separation/transfer of charge carriers and restrain the recombination of photogenerated electron–hole (e−–h+) pairs. This research provides a facile method for large-scale preparation of CQDs modified semiconductor photocatalysts that can potentially be used in environmental and energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The issues of environmental pollution and energy shortage caused by the rapid population growth and fast economic development have brought great challenges to human society. Among them, water pollution is considered to be one of the most serious and worrying issues and needs immediate and practical solutions. In order to meet the demand for clean water, several techniques have been used to remove organic contaminants and toxic heavy metal ions from polluted water, such as adsorption, membrane filtration, biodegradation and photodegradation [1–4]. As a green technology, solar-driven semiconductor photocatalysis may be one of the best solutions and is hopeful to play a significant role in water treatment.

Among many different semiconductor photocatalysts, titanium dioxide (TiO2) has been the most extensively investigated and utilized in many applications over the past few decades because of its strong oxidizing ability, nontoxicity, chemical stability and low cost [5,6]. For different kinds of TiO2 materials previously reported, commercial TiO2 (P25) is a mixed phase TiO2 material composed of 80% anatase phase TiO2 and 20% rutile phase TiO2, which is the most widely applied photocatalyst due to the excellent photoactivity and stability. Nevertheless, because of the inherently relatively wide band gap (3.0–3.2 eV), TiO2 is merely highly reactive under ultraviolet (UV) light or near-UV light, which accounts for just 4% of the sun’s spectrum that illuminates on the Earth’s surface [7]. Meanwhile, visible light accounts for 44% of sunlight but cannot be utilized effectively, so TiO2 photocatalysis driven by sunlight is greatly limited in related applications. Another limitation to the photocatalytic process of TiO2 is the unstable and rapid recombination of photogenerated electron–hole (e−–h+) pairs [8,9], which makes the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2 unsatisfactory. To effectively utilize visible light and hinder the recombination of photogenerated e− and h+, various methods have been used for modifying TiO2, such as dye sensitization, metal doping, metal/metal oxide loading, non-metal doping, morphology control and coupling with other semiconductors [10–15]. Recently, the use of carbon-based nanomaterials such as fullerene [16], carbon fiber [17], carbon nanotube [18], graphene [19] and graphene oxide [20] to ameliorate the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 in the visible range has received increasing attention.

Carbon quantum Dots (CQDs) are a novel fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials that combine the outstanding electronic properties of carbon-based materials with the special optical properties of quantum dots to make them different from other carbon nanomaterials or traditional semiconductor quantum dots [21]. Since the first discovery of CQDs in 2004 [22], owing to the unique properties such as low cytotoxicity, easy of modification, tunable luminescence emission, chemical inertness and excellent biocompatibility [23], they have been extensively and continuously researched in many different fields, including sensing [24], bioimaging [25], nanomedicine [26], photocatalysis [27,28], electrocatalysis [29], photovoltaic devices [30] and optoelectronics [31], etc. Like other carbon-based nanomaterials, the use of CQDs to modify TiO2 to enhance the photocatalytic performance has received growing concern. Recently, Yu et al. [32] showed that CQDs modified TiO2 composites exhibit ameliorated photocatalytic H2 evolution rate under visible-light irradiation. Pan et al. [33] deposited CQDs on TiO2 nanoparticles under hydrothermal environments, in which CQDs can expand the photoresponse range of the composites and suppress the recombination of excitons to ameliorate photocatalytic activity. Martins et al. [34] observed enhanced visible light absorption and improved charge transfer in P25/NCQD composites, and the obtained composites showed an ameliorated photocatalytic activity in photooxidation of NO and photodegradation of methylene blue (MB). Min et al. [35] reported that CQDs can increase the optical absorption of TiO2 in the visible region and promote the charge separation of TiO2, thereby improving the photocatalytic hydrogen evolution activity. Li et al. [36] confirmed that CQDs can promote the formation of H2O2 to consume holes, thereby improving the Cr(VI) photoreduction performance of CQDs-TiO2 nanosheets. In the above studies on the modification of TiO2 by CQDs, the CQDs were first prepared and purified, and then coupled with TiO2 by an additional hydrothermal treatment to form the composite or CQDs were directly produced and combined with TiO2 to form the composite under hydrothermal conditions. Although these prepared composites have better photocatalytic performance than pure TiO2, there are still lots of limitations from complex synthesis procedures to higher energy consumption requirements, such as harsh reagents/conditions (strong acid or alkali) [32,33,35], tedious post-processing purification procedures (1–3 days) [32,34,36] and external energy supply (130–200 °C) [32–36]. Among them, since it is difficult to completely remove excessive strong acids or alkalis during the synthesis process, additional environmental problems may be added. Therefore, there is still a great need to exploit a more facile method for mass production of CQD/TiO2 composite photocatalysts.

Coal tar pitch (CTP) is a cheap by-product of coal tar processing [37]. Very recently, considering the similarity in molecular structure between CTP and CQDs, we have used CTP as “natural CQDs” and prepared CQDs/TiO2 composites for visible-light photocatalysis by a solvothermal method [38]. In this work, we demonstrate a more facile impregnation method to fabricate effective CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts at room temperature, and study their photocatalytic activity on degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under the illumination of visible light. The XPS and FT-IR results of CQDs/TiO2 composites show that CQDs and TiO2 can be successfully coupled by forming Ti‒O‒C bonds. The CQDs/TiO2 composites possess higher photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation than pure TiO2. Our method does not require expensive raw materials, harsh reagents, complicated synthesis procedures, tedious post-treatment purification steps and any additional energy supply. To the best of our knowledge, this method should be the simplest preparation method of CQDs modified TiO2 composite photocatalysts, and can be extended to mass production of excellent modified semiconductor composite photocatalysts in a green way.

The original coal tar pitch was supplied by Henan Zhonghong Group Coal Chemical Corporation (Pingdingshan, China) and subjected to dry distillation at 400 °C to remove the light oil component for subsequent use. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%) was procured from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Corporation (China). Absolute ethanol (99.7%) was obtained from Luoyang Chemical Reagent Factory (China). The fine powdered TiO2 (P25) used was procured from Evonik Degussa (Germany). The disposable needle filters (aperture: 0.22 µm) were procured from Tianjin Jinteng Experiment Equipment Corporation (China). Ammonium oxalate (AO, 99.8%), p-benzoquinone (BQ, 97%), isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.5%) and RhB (99%) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Corporation (China). Deionized (DI) water was used in the next experiment (resistivity>18.1 MΩ). Each reagents was of analytical grade and was used directly without any treatment prior to use.

In a typical preparation method, 30 mg of CTP was firstly dispersed in DMF (40 mL) by ultrasonic treatment for 1 h. After insoluble substances were filtered off by disposable needle filters (0.22 µm), 0.5 g of fine powdered TiO2 particles was put into DMF. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 1 h and then subjected to magnetic stirring for 12 h at room temperature. Next, the obtained mixture was filtered through needle filters to remove insoluble substances (if present), and the final product was obtained by centrifugation (10,000 rpm) for 8 min, washing sequentially for several times using DI water and ethanol and drying at 60 °C overnight. The fabricated sample was named as CQDs/TiO2-6. The CQDs/TiO2 composites with different amounts of CQDs were obtained by simply adjusting the adding quality of CTP from 5 mg to 35 mg according to the same experimental procedure. The prepared CQDs/TiO2 composites with different CQDs contents are denoted to be CQDs/TiO2-1, CQDs/TiO2-3, CQDs/TiO2-5 and CQDs/TiO2-7, respectively. Besides, CTP powders were sonicated in DMF for 2 h, and the supernatant was collected by centrifugation and filtration to obtain CQDs solutions.

The morphology, size and lattice characteristics of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalyst were observed through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images obtained by a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission TEM (FEI, USA) with the acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were carried out on an X′Pert PRO diffractometer (Panalytical, Holland) with Cu Kα radiation (operating condition: 40 kV, 15 mA) in a continuous mode with a scanning range from 5° to 60° and a step size of 0.02°. Elemental mapping patterns was obtained using a Merlin Compact high resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, Carl Zeiss, Germany) with an energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) detector. Raman spectra of samples were recorded on an inVia Confocal Raman microscope (Renishaw, UK) with a spatial resolution of 1 µm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was obtained by an Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) employing monochromated Al Kα radiation (1486.6 eV, 150 W) as an X-ray source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted on a Tensor 27 instrument (Bruker, Germany) with a scanning range from 3600 to 400 cm−1. Using barium sulphate as the 100% reflectance standard, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of samples were conducted on a UH4150 instrument (Hitachi, Japan) having an integrating sphere with the sampling interval of 0.50 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra excited at 320 nm were performed through a Nanolog FL3-2iHR luminescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) utilizing a Xe lamp as the excitation source. Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm tests were carried out on an ASAP 2020 physical adsorption system (Micromeritics, USA). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and photocurrent response tests were recorded through a RST 5000 electrochemical analyzer (Suzhou Risetest Electronic Corporation, China) in a conventional three-electrode configuration with 0.5 M sodium sulfate aqueous solution as the electrolyte.

In a typical photocatalytic test, 100 mg of sample was dispersed in RhB aqueous solution (10 ppm, 100 mL), and the mixed solution was maintained in vigorous stirring in a dark environment for 1 h before irradiation to achieve the adsorption/desorption balance between RhB and photocatalysts. A 300 W Xe arc lamp with a cut-off filter (λ>420 nm) was employed as a light source. The mixed solution was then exposed to visible light and a small amount of the aliquots (ca. 5 mL) was taken out at a certain time interval (1 h) under magnetic stirring. The photocatalyst was precipitated from the mixed solution through centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, and the absorbance of supernatant at λmax = 554 nm was analyzed by a visible spectrophotometer (722S type, Shanghai Lengguang Technology Corporation, China) to calculate the concentration of RhB solution. After the photocatalytic experiment, the photocatalyst was recovered through centrifugation and then washed with DI water and absolute ethanol until the dye molecules adsorbed on the photocatalyst were completely desorbed back into the aqueous solution. The recovered photocatalyst was finally dried at 60 °C overnight for subsequent recycling tests.

For the sake of determining the contribution of reactive substances participated in the photocatalytic reaction, different free radicals scavenging experiments were carried out. Among them, 1 mM of AO, BQ and IPA was added as radical scavengers into catalyst-dye solution (100 mg, 10 ppm, 100 mL) to investigate the influence of holes (h+), superoxide radical (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (·OH) in the photocatalytic reaction, respectively. The photodegradation test of the dye-photocatalyst-quencher solution was then carried out in accordance with the exact same experimental procedure as mentioned in the preceding section.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The shape and size of the obtained CQDs are investigated by means of TEM, as displayed in Fig. 1a. After randomly measuring 50 CQDs by TEM, it is found that the diameters of CQDs are mainly distributed in the scope of 4.3–11.1 nm, and the mean diameter is about 6.8 nm (size distribution is displayed in the insert in Fig. 1a). The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) indicates that most of the obtained CQDs have a distinct lattice fringe with an interplanar distance of 0.212 nm matching with the (100) crystalline face of graphitic carbon. From the representative TEM image of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite (Fig. 1c), it can be concluded that CQDs are homogeneously decorated on the surface of TiO2. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1d) further confirms that CQDs and TiO2 are coupled to form the composite, in which the lattice fringes distance of 0.211 nm and 0.350 nm is assigned to the (100) crystalline face of graphitic carbon and the (101) crystalline face of anatase TiO2, respectively. Besides, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. S1) shows distinct diffraction planes corresponding to the (101), (004), (105), (220) and (215) crystal planes of anatase TiO2, respectively. The elemental mapping patterns of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite ( Fig. 2) show the homogeneous distribution of Ti, O and C elements, revealing that CQDs are uniformly deposited on the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles.

The XRD pattern of CQDs (Fig. S2) reveals a wide peak centered at ~ 24.5°, suggesting the (002) plane of graphite carbon [39]. Fig. 3a reveals the XRD patterns of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites having different CQDs contents. In the patterns, the peaks at 25.56°, 38.06°, 48.32°, 54.18° and 55.28° are assigned to (101), (004), (200), (105) and (211) crystal planes of anatase phase TiO2 (JCPDS card 21-1272), and the peaks at 27.70°, 36.30° and 41.56° are assigned to (110), (101) and (111) crystal planes of rutile phase TiO2 (JCPDS card 21-1276). It is worth noting that with the gradual increase of CQDs loading, the (101) peak of all CQD/TiO2 composites gradually shifts to a smaller angle than pure TiO2, indicating that the lattice structure of TiO2 is slightly distorted due to the interaction with CQDs [40]. Besides, no typical XRD peaks associated with carbon are observed in the CQDs/TiO2 composites, which can be interpreted by the low content, well dispersion and poor crystallinity of CQDs [41]. Fig. 3b displays the Raman spectra of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. For pure TiO2, the four characteristic Raman bands at 142.8, 395.1, 517.0 and 637.1 cm−1 are related to Eg(1), B1g(1), A1g + B1g(2) and Eg(3) vibration modes of anatase TiO2, respectively [35]. It can be seen in CQDS that two characteristic Raman bands corresponding to the D and G bands of CQDS are detected at 1359 and 1589 cm−1, respectively. In CQDs/TiO2-6 composite, not only the four characteristic bands of TiO2 are identified, but also the two characteristic bands of CQDs are observed, which demonstrates the existence of CQDs in the CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. In addition, the Raman Eg(1) peak positions of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite are compared (Fig. S3). The Raman Eg(1) peak of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite is located at 147.8 cm−1, which is shifted by 5 cm−1 from that of TiO2. The result shows that there is a strong interaction between CQDs and TiO2, and the shift of the Raman Eg(1) peak in the composite may be due to the formation of the Ti‒O‒C bonds[42].

The functional groups of CQDs, TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite are analyzed through FT-IR characterization, as displayed in Fig. 3c. With respect to CQDs, the absorption peaks at 1028, 1613, 2921 and 3437 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C‒O, C═O, H-Csp3 and O‒H, respectively. Moreover, the band at 742 cm−1 is relevant to the out-of-plane bending vibration of H-Csp2. The broad absorption peaks around 3340 and 1635 cm−1 exhibited in both TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 samples are related to the stretching vibration of the adsorbed water molecules and O‒H bonds [42]. The peaks at 610 and 655 cm−1 in CQDs/TiO2-6 and TiO2 samples are related to Ti‒O and O‒Ti‒O bonds, respectively [43]. And an absorption peak associated with the Ti‒O‒Ti bond is observed at 1383 cm−1 in CQDs/TiO2-6 composite [42]. Beyond that, there are two new peaks related to the C‒O and C═C stretching vibrations at 1037 and 1514 cm−1 in CQDs/TiO2-6 composite, which proves the existence of CQDs in the composite. Even more important, the broad absorption below 1000 cm−1 in CQDs/TiO2-6 composite shifts to high wavenumber in contrast to pure TiO2, illustrating that there is a good chemical connection between the two components by forming Ti‒O‒C bonds [34,36].

XPS measurements are employed to further assess the surface chemical compositions and elemental chemical states in CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. It is determined by the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3d) that the CQDs/TiO2-6 composite contains three main elements of Ti (21.23%), O (43.30%), C (34.72%) and negligible N elements (0.75%), wherein the presence of C and N elements indicates that CQDs exist on the surface of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite. Fig. 3e displays the C 1s XPS spectrum of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite with three binding energy peaks. The binding energy peaks of 284.8, 285.6 and 288.7 eV can be designated to C‒C/C═C, C‒O and C═O bonds, respectively, indicating that CQDs are present on the CQDs/TiO2-6 composite’s surface. The O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3f) displays that the two fitted peaks of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite located at 530.0 and 530.8 eV can be matched to Ti‒O and C‒O/C═O bonds. The Ti 2p XPS spectrum displays that CQDs/TiO2-6 composite has two binding energy peaks, and the peaks at 458.9 eV and 464.6 eV are relevant to Ti (2p 3/2) and Ti (2p 1/2). It is noteworthy that the Ti 2p and O 1s peaks of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite shift toward higher binding energies in contrast to pure TiO2. The Ti 2p peaks shift of 0.2–0.4 eV and the O 1s peaks shift of 0.4–0.8 eV suggest that the bonding environments of Ti and O in CQDs/TiO2-6 composite change, indicating that the chemically bonded composite is eventually formed by forming Ti‒O‒C bonds between CQDs and TiO2 [32,34].

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and BJH pore size distributions of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composites are shown in Fig. 4, and the textural parameters of the samples are summarized in Table S1. All of them exhibit the characteristic type IV isotherms, demonstrating the presence of mesoporous structure [18]. The corresponding BET specific surface area of pure TiO2 is measured to be 49.42 m2/g. As the loading of CQDs increases from 1% to 6%, the specific surface area of the CQDs/TiO2 composites increase from 49.81 m2/g to 54.07 m2/g. However, further increasing the loading of CQDs leads to a slight decrease in the specific surface area probably because a further increase in the content of CQDs leads to the aggregation of CQDs to form large particles.

The light absorption ability of photocatalysts is studied by DRS, as displayed in Fig. 5a. Pure TiO2 has almost no absorption in the visible range and can only absorb light with the wavelength less than 402 nm. After the introduction of CQDs, the absorption edge of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts expands from 431 to 470 nm, which means that CQDs act as a crucial role in prolonging the light response region of photocatalysts. The CQDs/TiO2 composites display enhanced visible-light absorption capacity relative to pure TiO2, and their absorption intensities increase with the increase of CQDs content, which is the same as the previously reported researches on carbon nanomaterials modified TiO2 [18,20,35,36].

In the meantime, the band gap (Eg) of photocatalysts is calculated through the modified Kubelka–Munk equation [44], as presented in Fig. 5b. The band gaps of pure TiO2, CQDs/TiO2-1, CQDs/TiO2-3, CQDs/TiO2-5, CQDs/TiO2-6 and CQDs/TiO2-7 are estimated to be 3.07, 2.89, 2.86, 2.84, 2.77 and 2.68 eV, respectively. In contrast to pure TiO2, the band gap of the composites decreases with the increase of the amount of CQDs introduced, which is caused by the formation of Ti‒O‒C bonds in CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts [20]. Since the photocatalysts exhibit enhanced visible-light absorption by narrowing the band gap, it is hoped that the CQDs/TiO2 composites can make better use of visible light and get better applications in related fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption property of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite for RhB in the dark environment was investigated before irradiation. The result shows that the adsorption/desorption equilibrium is established in about 40 min (Fig. S4). Based on this result, the adsorption/desorption balance can be reached between the photocatalyst and RhB by vigorously stirring the mixture in the dark for 1 h before irradiation. Fig. 6a presents the photocatalytic activity of various photocatalysts for degradation of RhB under the illumination of visible light, and Fig. 6b displays the histogram of the degradation efficiency (%) of RhB for different photocatalysts. After 4 h of irradiation, RhB is self-degraded by 3% and only 6% of RhB is degraded in the existence of TiO2. The photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 composites is remarkably improved by comparison with TiO2, and the CQDs/TiO2-6 composite has the best photodegradation performance. The degradation efficiency of all photocatalysts observes the order as CQDs/TiO2-6 (93.4%)>CQDs/TiO2-7 (74.2%)>CQDs/TiO2-5 (64.0%)>CQDs/TiO2-3 (55.5%)>CQDs/TiO2-1 (51.3%)>TiO2 (6.0%), illustrating that the proper CQDs loading can noticeably ameliorate the photocatalytic performance of TiO2.

The kinetic curves for RhB degradation of photocatalysts under visible-light irradiation are displayed in Fig. 6c, which shows that the plots of −ln(C/C0) against irradiation time (t) can be well linearly fitted with good correlation coefficients (R2 ≥ 0.96), suggesting that the reaction kinetics follow the pseudo first order model. The degradation rate constants are acquired by calculating the slope of the fitted straight lines. As displayed in Fig. 6d, the degradation rate constant is 0.0082 h−1 in the absence of any photocatalyst and the degradation rate constant is 0.0152 h−1 in the presence of TiO2. With regard to CQDs/TiO2-1, CQDs/TiO2-3, CQDs/TiO2-5, CQDs/TiO2-6 and CQDs/TiO2-7 composite, the degradation rate constants are determined to be 0.1824, 0.2048, 0.2542, 0.6637 and 0.3387 h−1, respectively. The CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts possess much higher degradation rate constants than pure TiO2 (12.0–43.7 fold higher), where the sample with optimal CQDs content (6 wt%) has the optimum photocatalytic activity. The experimental findings illustrate that the introduction of appropriate CQDs content possess a positive impact on the photocatalytic reaction.

The photocatalytic degradation of RhB solution on the optimum composite photocatalyst (CQDs/TiO2-6) was studied under three pH values of the dye solution, as shown in Fig. S5. The results show that 93.4% of RhB is degraded at pH 7 after 4 h of visible-light irradiation. However, the photocatalytic degradation rate at pH 3 is 72.7%, and the degradation rate at pH 10 decreases from 93.4% to 46.3%. At pH values less than or greater than PZC (pHpzc = 3.3), the surface charges of TiO2 are positive and negative, respectively [45]. When the solution pH (pH = 7) is greater than the PZC (pHPZC = 3.3) of TiO2, the photocatalyst has good photocatalytic performance. In this environment, RhB is in cationic form (RhB+) and the surface charge of the photocatalyst is negative, so TiO2 can attract dyes and degrade most of them [45]. When the pH value is reduced from 7 to 3, the photodegradation rate of RhB decreases. At the pH lower than pHPZC, the dyes exist in the form of cation and the surface charge of TiO2 is positive [45]; therefore, due to electrostatic repulsion, the dye molecules are difficult to adsorb on the surface of the photocatalyst during the degradation process. At pH = 10, the dye is less degraded. This may be because the dye is in the form of zwitterions which in turn increases the possible dimerization of RhB in water due to the attractive electrostatic attraction between the negative and the positive heads [45]. Therefore, due to the decrease in charge density, there is almost no attraction between the dye and TiO2. At the same time, the repulsive force between the negative surface of TiO2 and the negative head of the dye leads to the reduction of the degradation rate [45]. The effect of pH of the solution on the photocatalytic degradation of RhB is consistent with the results of Isari et al. [46] and Shafaee et al. [45].

The CQDs/TiO2-6 composite photocatalyst after RhB degradation experiment was collected for recycling testing to study the reusability and stability of the composite photocatalyst. After four consecutive cycles of photocatalytic degradation experiments, the composite is not remarkably deactivated under visible-light irradiation, which still retain 85% of RhB degradation efficiency ( Fig. 7a). As displayed in Fig. 7b, the XRD characterization of CQDs/TiO2-6 composite photocatalyst before and after the four-cycle experiment does not show marked changes in the crystalline phase and structure of the composite, indicating that the composite photocatalyst is stable even after the fourth round of photodegradation experiments. The results show that the photocatalyst has good reusability and structural stability, which is advantageous for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism with enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite
ce:section.ce:para: The above results of RhB photodegradation show that the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 is significantly improved when combined with CQDs, which proves that CQDs act as a vital role in enhancing the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 composites.

It is universally acknowledged that photocatalytic performance is closely connected with the separation and transfer efficiency of photoinduced carriers in the photocatalyst. CQDs have been proved to have good electron storage capability; therefore, photogenerated e− from TiO2 can shuttle in the conducting network of CQDs [21], thereby inhibiting the recombination of photogenerated e− and h+ and improving the photocatalytic activity. This can be demonstrated by PL and EIS measurements. Fig. 8a presents the PL spectra of pure TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts excited at 320 nm. The stronger the PL intensity, the higher the recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers [46]. By comparing the PL spectra of all photocatalysts, it is found that all CQDs/TiO2 composites display pronounced attenuation in PL intensity by comparison with pure TiO2, illustrating that the loading of CQDs can effectively hinder the recombination of photogenerated e− and h+. Among them, CQDs/TiO2-6 composite exhibits the lowest PL intensity, indicating the least recombination rate of photogenerated e− and h+.

EIS analysis is served to investigate the transfer and separation of photoinduced charge carriers, as displayed in Fig. 8b. In general, the smaller arc radius reflects the faster interfacial charge transfer and the higher separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers [47]. In EIS Nyquist plots, the order of the relative arc radius is TiO2> CQDs/TiO2-1 > CQDs/TiO2-3 > CQDs/TiO2-5 > CQDs/TiO2-7 > CQDs/TiO2-6. It can be concluded that when CQDs are employed to modify TiO2, the interface charge transfer is faster and the separation efficiency of the photoinduced charge carriers is higher than that of pure TiO2. The PL and EIS results show that as the CQDs content increases, the charge carriers’ recombination rate decreases first and then increases. The CQDs/TiO2-6 composite has the lowest recombination rate of photoinduced charge carriers, thus conducing to the optimal photocatalytic activity. Excess CQDs can act as recombination centers of charge carriers, bringing about a decrease in photocatalytic performance [48], which is in line with the results of RhB photodegradation. Moreover, in order to more intuitively comprehend the separation/transfer of photoinduced charge carriers, the transient photocurrent responses of several on-off cycles of TiO2 and CQDs/TiO2-6 composite are recorded under the illumination of visible light (λ > 420 nm) and plotted in Fig. S6. Unlike pure TiO2, which has only a very slight photocurrent density (0.33 μA/cm2), the CQDs/TiO2-6 composite exhibits a remarkable photocurrent response (0.90 μA/cm2). This result means that CQDs act as an important role in the separation/transfer process of photoinduced charge carriers, and can effectively hinder the recombination of photoinduced e− and h+, which is in accordance with the findings of PL and EIS analyses.

It is universally acknowledged that the three major active substances participated in the photodegradation of dye molecules are photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and superoxide radical (·O2 −). For the sake of investigating the contribution of three active species in photocatalytic reaction, ammonium oxalate (AO), isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) are employed as scavengers for h+, ·OH and ·O2 −, respectively. In contrast with the absence of any free radical scavengers, the photocatalytic activity of composite photocatalyst is inhibited to some extent when these three scavengers are added ( Fig. 9a). As you can see from Fig. 9b that the degradation efficiency of RhB decreases from 93.4% to 29.8% when AO is added, demonstrating that h+ is the primary active substance during the photocatalytic reaction. Similarly, the degradation efficiency of RhB also shows a remarkable decrease after the addition of IPA, illustrating that ·OH radical also plays a significant role in photodegradation of RhB. However, RhB is degraded by 71.3% in the presence of BQ, suggesting that ·O2 − radical has little effect on the photodegradation process. Hence, the contribution of three oxidative species participated in the degradation of RhB molecules is as follows: holes (h+)>hydroxyl radical (·OH)>superoxide radical (·O2 −).

On the basis of above results, the possible mechanism for improving the photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalyst is described in Fig. 10. Firstly, the successful coupling of TiO2 and CQDs can extend the photoresponsive range of TiO2 from UV to visible region and reduce the band gap of TiO2, thereby improving the utilization efficiency of visible light and leading to the improvement of photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composites. Secondly, CQDs have been reported to act as e− reservoirs and donors to capture photogenerated e− from the conduction band of TiO2 during photodegradation [32,36,49], accelerate the separation/transfer of photoinduced charge carriers and suppress the recombination of photogenerated e− and h+, consequently ameliorating the photocatalytic performance. This charge separation process promotes the formation of ·OH radical and ·O2 − radical. In the light of the previous report [42], holes are a source of ·OH radical during photodegradation. And e− from CQDs are migrated to the conduction band of TiO2, trapping the molecular oxygen to produce ·O2 − radical [42]. However, ·O2 − radical is not stable in aqueous solution and can be rapidly converted to hydroxyl radical [50]. The RhB molecules adsorbed on the surface of CQDs/TiO2 composite are oxidized and decomposed into CO2 and H2O by ·OH radical generated by the reaction of h+ and ·O2 − radical formed by photoinduced e−. The possible mechanism of RhB degradation process on the composite photocatalysts is illustrated as follows:
(1) CQDs/TiO2 + hν → e− + h+
(2) H2O + h+ → H+ + ·OH
(3) O2 + e− → ·O2 −
(4) O2 − + 2H+ → H2O2
(5) H2O2 → 2·OH
(6) OH + RhB → CO2 + H2O
(7) O2 − + RhB → CO2 + H2O

Table 1 compares the results of this work with the photocatalytic activity of other CQDs/TiO2 composites. The results show that the preparation method in this work is the simplest (does not require harsh reagents, complicated synthesis procedures and any additional energy supply) and the prepared photocatalyst has good photocatalytic performance.

In conclusion, the CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts were successfully prepared at room temperature by a simple energy-efficient impregnation method. The characterization results reveal that coupling CQDs to the surface of TiO2 nanoparticles can not only enhance the absorption of visible light but also effectively hinder the recombination of photoinduced electron–hole pairs. The optimized CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts show significantly improved photocatalytic activity, and the degradation rate constant is 43.7 times that of pure TiO2. The superior photocatalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 composites can be attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs, which can act as a photosensitizer to improve the utilization efficiency of visible light, but also act as an e− effective reservoir and donor to promote the separation/transfer of photogenerated charge carrier of TiO2. The current research provides a simple method for the possible large-scale development of semiconductor composite photocatalysts. In the future, it will be necessary to further improve the photocatalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts by modification and apply them to other photocatalytic fields, such as photocatalytic removal of nitrogen oxides/toxic heavy metal ions and photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Jingjie Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data Curation, Writing - original draft. Quanrun Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding Acquisition, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Junhao Wang: Resources, Investigation, Software. He He: Resources, Validation. Feng Shi: Resources, Validation. Baolin Xing: Resources. Jianbo Jia: Resources. Guangxu Huang: Resources. Chuanxiang Zhang: Funding acquisition, Project Administration, Supervision, Writing – Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful for the financial support given by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant numbers U1361119, and 51404098).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jallcom.2021.159389.
